problem_statement: Let ABC be a triangle with circumcentre O, centroid G, orthocentre H, and de Longchamps point L (the reflection of H in O). Prove that L lies on the Euler line at three times the distance of the centroid from O, i.e. O, G, L are collinear and OL = 3*OG.

construction_steps.name: H
construction_steps.label: H
construction_steps.command: Intersect(PerpendicularLine(A, Line(B, C)), PerpendicularLine(B, Line(C, A)))

problem_statement: Let ABC be a triangle with circumcentre O, incentre I, and Bevan point V (the reflection of I in O). Let Ge be the centroid of the excentral triangle IaIbIc, namely (Ia + Ib + Ic)/3. Prove that V, O, I and Ge are collinear (they all lie on the line OI).

construction_steps.name: I
construction_steps.label: I
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

construction_steps.name: Ia
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

construction_steps.name: Ib
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, B, A), PerpendicularLine(C, AngleBisector(B, C, A)))

construction_steps.name: Ic
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(A, C, B), PerpendicularLine(A, AngleBisector(C, A, B)))

problem_statement: Let ABC be a triangle with Lemoine (symmedian) point K, and let Ka, Kb, Kc be the feet of the perpendiculars from K to the sides BC, CA, AB. Prove that the perpendicular distances from K to the three sides are proportional to the lengths of those sides, i.e. KKa : KKb : KKc = BC : CA : AB.

construction_steps.name: K
construction_steps.label: K
construction_steps.command: Intersect(Reflect(Line(A, Midpoint(B, C)), AngleBisector(B, A, C)), Reflect(Line(B, Midpoint(C, A)), AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

problem_statement: Let ABC be a triangle with incentre I. Let the A-mixtilinear incircle (tangent to AB, to AC, and internally tangent to the circumcircle) touch the circumcircle at the point T. Let M be the midpoint of the arc BAC (the arc BC that contains A). Prove that the points T, I and M are collinear.

construction_steps.name: I
construction_steps.label: I
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

construction_steps.name: P
construction_steps.command: Intersect(Line(A, B), PerpendicularLine(I, AngleBisector(B, A, C)))

construction_steps.name: Oa
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), PerpendicularLine(P, Line(A, B)))

problem_statement: Let ABC be a triangle with incentre I, and let the A-mixtilinear incircle (tangent to AB, AC, and internally tangent to the circumcircle) touch AB at P and AC at Q. Prove that AP = AQ, i.e. the two tangent segments from A to the mixtilinear incircle are equal.

construction_steps.name: I
construction_steps.label: I
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

construction_steps.name: P
construction_steps.label: P
construction_steps.command: Intersect(Line(A, B), PerpendicularLine(I, AngleBisector(B, A, C)))

construction_steps.name: Q
construction_steps.label: Q
construction_steps.command: Intersect(Line(A, C), PerpendicularLine(I, AngleBisector(B, A, C)))

construction_steps.name: Oa
construction_steps.label: O_a
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), PerpendicularLine(P, Line(A, B)))

problem_statement: Let ABC be a triangle with incentre I, and let the A-mixtilinear incircle touch AB at P and AC at Q. Prove that the chord of contact PQ passes through the incentre I and is perpendicular to the line AI.

construction_steps.name: I
construction_steps.label: I
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

construction_steps.name: P
construction_steps.label: P
construction_steps.command: Intersect(Line(A, B), PerpendicularLine(I, AngleBisector(B, A, C)))

construction_steps.name: Q
construction_steps.label: Q
construction_steps.command: Intersect(Line(A, C), PerpendicularLine(I, AngleBisector(B, A, C)))

problem_statement: Let ABC be a triangle with circumcentre O and symmedian point K. Construct the first Brocard point Om1 (angle Om1AB = angle Om1BC = angle Om1CA). Prove that Om1 lies on the circle with diameter OK (the Brocard circle), i.e. angle O Om1 K is a right angle.

construction_steps.name: K
construction_steps.label: K
construction_steps.command: Intersect(Reflect(Line(A, Midpoint(B, C)), AngleBisector(B, A, C)), Reflect(Line(B, Midpoint(C, A)), AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

construction_steps.name: cB
construction_steps.command: Circle(Intersect(PerpendicularBisector(A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, Line(B, C))), A)

construction_steps.name: cC
construction_steps.command: Circle(Intersect(PerpendicularBisector(B, C), PerpendicularLine(C, Line(C, A))), B)

problem_statement: Let ABC be a triangle with incentre I and circumcircle Gamma of centre O and radius R. Construct the A-mixtilinear incircle as the circle whose centre Oa lies on the internal bisector of angle A and which is tangent to AB at the point P where the perpendicular to AI at I meets AB. Prove that this circle is internally tangent to the circumcircle Gamma, i.e. the distance OOa equals R minus the mixtilinear radius OaP.

construction_steps.name: I
construction_steps.label: I
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

construction_steps.name: P
construction_steps.label: P
construction_steps.command: Intersect(Line(A, B), PerpendicularLine(I, AngleBisector(B, A, C)))

construction_steps.name: Oa
construction_steps.label: O_a
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), PerpendicularLine(P, Line(A, B)))

problem_statement: Let c1 = Circle(O1,2) with O1=(0,0) and c2 = Circle(O2,3) with O2=(5,0) be externally tangent (O1O2 = 5 = r1 + r2). A common external tangent touches c1 at A and c2 at B. Prove that the external tangent segment AB has length 2·sqrt(r1·r2) = 2·sqrt(6), with O1A ⟂ AB and O2B ⟂ AB.

Let two lines l1 = Line(A,B) and l2 = Line(A,C) meet at A=(0,0) with B=(6,0), C=(2,5). Let O be a point on the bisector of angle BAC and let F1, F2 be the feet of the perpendiculars from O to l1 and l2. Prove that OF1 = OF2, so the circle centered at O tangent to l1 at F1 is also tangent to l2 at F2.

Let c = Circle(O,2) with O=(0,0). The director circle (orthoptic) of c is Circle(O, 2·sqrt(2)). Let P be a point on the director circle. Prove that the two tangents from P to c are perpendicular to each other (P sees c under a right angle), i.e. PT1 ⟂ PT2 where T1, T2 are the points of tangency.

Let c = Circle(O,2.5) with O=(0,0), and P=(4,2), Q=(-1,5) outside c. Let pP be the polar of P and pQ the polar of Q, meeting at X. Prove (La Hire) that X is the pole of line PQ, equivalently OX ⟂ PQ and the polar of X is exactly the line PQ.

Let c1 = Circle(O1,2) with O1=(-4,-1) and c2 = Circle(O2,3) with O2=(5,2). Let I be the internal center of similitude of c1 and c2. Prove that I divides segment O1O2 internally in the ratio O1I : IO2 = r1 : r2 = 2 : 3, equivalently 3·(I - O1) = 2·(O2 - I).

Let A, B, C, D lie in order on Circle(O,4) with O=(0,0): A=(4,0), B = Rotate(A,70°,O), C = Rotate(A,155°,O), D = Rotate(A,250°,O). The diagonals AC and BD meet at P. Prove the diagonal-ratio relation PA / PC = (AB·AD) / (CB·CD).

Let c1 = Circle(O1,3) with O1=(0,0) and c2 = Circle(O2,4) with O2=(5,0), so that |O1O2|² = 25 = 9 + 16 = r1² + r2². Prove that c1 and c2 are orthogonal: at each intersection point X the radii O1X and O2X are perpendicular (so the circles meet at right angles).

Let c = Circle(O,2) with O=(1,1) and an external point P=(7,5). The two tangents from P touch c at T1, T2; let M be the midpoint of T1T2. Prove that O, M, P are collinear and OM·OP = 4 (= r²), so M is the inverse of P in c.

Let c1 = Circle(O1,1.5) with O1=(-3,0) and c2 = Circle(O2,3) with O2=(4,1). Let S be the external center of similitude. Prove that a common external tangent passes through S: the tangent from S to c1 (touching at U1) is also tangent to c2 (touching at U2), so S, U1, U2 are collinear.

Let c = Circle(O,3) with O=(0,0), and let chord AB have endpoints A=(3,0) and B = Rotate(A,110°,O). The tangents to c at A and B meet at the pole P of chord AB. Prove that OP ⟂ AB and the intersection M of OP with AB satisfies OM·OP = 9 (= r²), so M and P are inverse points in c.

Let c1 = Circle(O1,2) with O1=(0,0) and c2 = Circle(O2,3) with O2=(5,0) be externally tangent at T. A line through T meets c1 again at X and c2 again at Y. Prove that the radii O1X and O2Y are parallel (T is the internal homothety center of the two circles), so the tangents to c1 at X and to c2 at Y are parallel.

Let B=(-4,0), C=(3,0). For ratio k=1.5 the Apollonius circle of B,C is the locus PB/PC=1.5, with diameter endpoints D (internal, BD/DC=1.5) and E (external) on BC; let Oa be its center. Prove that B and C are inverse points with respect to this Apollonius circle: Oa, B, C are collinear and Oa B·Oa C = r² (r the radius).

Let l = Line(F, F+(1,0)) be a horizontal line with F=(0,0), and let G=(2,4) be a point off l. The circle tangent to l at F and passing through G has its center on the vertical line through F at the intersection with the perpendicular bisector of FG. Build this center Oc and circle; prove the circle is tangent to l at F (Oc F ⟂ l and |Oc F| = |Oc G| = radius).

Let c1 = Circle(O1,2) with O1=(-3,0) and c2 = Circle(O2,1.5) with O2=(4,0), disjoint. Let I be the internal center of similitude. Prove that a common internal tangent passes through I: the tangent from I to c1 (touching at U) is also tangent to c2 (touching at V), so I, U, V are collinear.
In triangle ABC with AB = 13, BC = 14, CA = 15, let O be the circumcenter. Determine the distance from O to line BC. (Answer: 4.125.)
In triangle ABC with AB = 13, BC = 14, CA = 15, let H be the orthocenter. Determine the distance AH from vertex A to H. (Answer: AH = 8.25.)
In triangle ABC, angle A = 120 degrees, AB = 3, and AC = 5. Determine the length of BC. (Answer: BC = 7.)
In triangle ABC with A=(1,3), B=(0,0), C=(2,0) (so AB^2+AC^2 = 5*BC^2), let N be the midpoint of AC and L the midpoint of AB. Prove that the medians BN and CL are perpendicular.
A circle has center O and radius 5. Point P satisfies OP = 13. Determine the length of the tangent from P to the circle. (Answer: 12.)
Two externally tangent circles have radii 4 and 9 (centers O1, O2 with O1O2 = 13). A common external tangent touches them at T1 and T2. Determine the length T1T2. (Answer: 12.)
Trapezoid ABCD has AB parallel to CD with AB = 10 and CD = 6. The diagonals AC and BD meet at P. Determine the ratio AP:PC. (Answer: 5:3 = 1.6667.)
In triangle ABC, AB = 13, BC = 14, CA = 15. Determine the circumradius R of triangle ABC. (Answer: R = 65/8 = 8.125.)
In triangle ABC, angle B = 2*angle C, with angle C = 35 degrees (so angle B = 70, angle A = 75) and BC = 10. Show that CA^2 = AB*(AB + BC), and determine CA. (Answer: CA = 9.72841...)
Triangle ABC has a right angle at A with AB = 5 and AC = 12 (so BC = 13). Determine the inradius r of triangle ABC. (Answer: r = 2.)

Let ABC be an equilateral triangle of side 6 and P an interior point (taken as the centroid for the construction). The sum of the distances from P to the three sides is constant. Determine this sum. (Answer: 3*sqrt(3) = 5.19615...)

Let ABCD be a tangential quadrilateral with incircle centre O and radius r (each side tangent to the circle), and let the incircle touch sides AB, BC, CD, DA at W, X, Y, Z. Prove that the two chords WY and XZ joining the contact points of opposite sides, together with the diagonal AC, are concurrent (all three pass through a single point P).

Let ABCD be a convex quadrilateral and P an arbitrary point. Let P1, P2, P3, P4 be the reflections of P in the midpoints of the sides AB, BC, CD, DA respectively. Prove that P1P2P3P4 is a parallelogram: the midpoint of diagonal P1P3 coincides with the midpoint of diagonal P2P4.

Let ABCD be a convex quadrilateral. Let G1 be the centroid of triangle ABD and G2 the centroid of triangle CBD. Prove that the segment G1G2 is parallel to the diagonal AC, and that its length is one third of AC.

Let ABCD be a parallelogram (A, B, D free with C = B + D - A). On each side erect a square externally with centres P, Q, R, S (on AB, BC, CD, DA). Prove Thebault's theorem: PQRS is a square, i.e. its diagonals PR and QS are equal and perpendicular AND its sides are equal, PQ = QR.

Let ABCD be a cyclic quadrilateral inscribed in a circle with centre O. Let G = (A+B+C+D)/4 be the centroid of its vertices, and let T be the point where the perpendicular from the midpoint of AB to side CD meets the perpendicular from the midpoint of BC to side DA (the anticentre). Prove that T is the reflection of the circumcentre O in the centroid G; equivalently G is the midpoint of OT.

Let ABCD be a tangential quadrilateral with incircle centre O and radius r (each side tangent to the circle), and let the incircle touch sides AB and BC at W and X respectively. Prove the equal-tangent property at vertex B: the two tangent segments from B to the points of tangency are equal, BW = BX.

Let ABCD be an orthodiagonal quadrilateral, constructed so that its diagonals AC and BD are perpendicular (A, C on one line through E, and B, D on the perpendicular line through E). Let P, Q, R, S be the midpoints of the sides AB, BC, CD, DA. Prove that the Varignon parallelogram PQRS is a rectangle: its adjacent sides PQ and QR are perpendicular.

Let ABCD be a convex quadrilateral with diagonals AC and BD. Let P, Q be the midpoints of sides AB, BC. Prove that the Varignon side PQ is parallel to the diagonal AC and equal to half of it: PQ is parallel to AC and PQ = AC/2.

Let ABCD be a convex quadrilateral. Let P, Q, R, S be the midpoints of the sides AB, BC, CD, DA, and let M, N be the midpoints of the diagonals AC, BD. Prove that the two bimedians PR and QS and the segment MN joining the diagonal-midpoints are all concurrent and bisect one another: the midpoints of PR, of QS and of MN all coincide.

Let ABCD be a parallelogram (A, B, D free with C = B + D - A). On each side erect a square externally with centres P, Q, R, S (on AB, BC, CD, DA). Prove that the centre of the square PQRS coincides with the centre of the parallelogram ABCD; that is, the midpoint of PR equals the midpoint of AC.

Let ABCD be an isosceles trapezoid with AB parallel to CD and legs AD = BC. Prove that the four vertices A, B, C, D are concyclic (an isosceles trapezoid is always a cyclic quadrilateral): there is a circle through A, B, C whose centre O is equidistant from D as well.

Let ABCD be a convex quadrilateral. Let Ga be the centroid of triangle BCD and Gd the centroid of triangle ABC. Prove that the segment GdGa is parallel to the side DA and that its length is one third of DA, GdGa = DA/3 (a consequence of the centroid quadrilateral being a homothetic copy of ABCD with ratio -1/3).

Let ABCD be a tangential quadrilateral with incircle centre O and radius r, touching sides AB, BC, CD, DA at W, X, Y, Z. Prove that the diagonal BD, together with the chords WY and XZ of contact points of opposite sides, are concurrent at a single point.
